text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Yesterday we looked at the table itself; the acacia wood pointing to the incorruptible character of Christ as a man, the pure gold pointing to the divine character with which He walked, and the gold crown pointing to His kingship that keeps His people from falling. Today we open what sat on top of the table; the twelve loaves of showbread themselves, the frankincense on top of them, and the Sabbath renewal. And we will find that the bread tells the same story as the table, but in even more detail.

The twelve loaves were made from the finest grade of wheat flour; not ordinary flour, but flour that had been sifted many times over until every lump, every coarse grain, every impurity and empty husk had been removed. To produce fine flour at all, the wheat had to go through a long and demanding process.
It was sown into the ground and buried. It grew through wind and rain and heat. It was reaped, threshed, winnowed, ground between millstones, and sifted again and again until what remained was perfectly smooth and even, without a single rough grain. Only after all of that was there fine flour.

This fine flour speaks of the character of Christ proved perfect under every process. There was no coarseness (roughness) in Him, no roughness,
no unevenness, no hidden flaw that came to the surface under pressure.
He was exactly the same in private as in public, the same to the person who loved Him as to the person who hated Him, the same in the peaceful hillsides of Galilee as in the hostile courts of Jerusalem. He was tested by Satan in the wilderness for forty days. He was tested by the Pharisees, the scribes, the Sadducees, the lawyers; every kind of religious and intellectual challenge thrown at Him. He was tested by suffering, by rejection, by the cross itself.
And after every process, not one grain of sin was found in Him. Hebrews 4:15: “In every respect He has been tempted as we are, yet without sin.” The grinding did not produce the purity. It proved what was already there.

In John 12:24 Christ spoke of Himself as a grain of wheat: “Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.” He was the grain that was sown and buried. He was the wheat that was threshed and ground. And what came out of the process the bread that feeds the world forever. The fine flour on the table began as a seed. Christ began as a baby born in a Bethlehem. Both went through everything the world could throw at them. Both came out as bread.

The showbread had to be unleavened; baked without yeast and baked quickly enough that not even the natural leaven in the air could get into the dough.
The result was flat, dense bread, without any rising or puffing up. In Scripture, leaven is consistently the picture of sin, corruption, and false teaching. Leviticus 2:11 forbids leaven from any offering made to Yahweh by fire. Matthew 16:6 uses leaven for the corrupting influence of false doctrine. 1 Corinthians 5:6–8 uses leaven for the hidden moral corruption that spreads through a community if it is allowed to remain.

The unleavened bread speaks of the complete sinlessness of Christ; not merely the absence of sinful actions, but the absence of any corrupting principle within Him. Leaven works from the inside out. It spreads invisibly through the whole lump. You cannot see it working, but the whole batch is affected. There was no leaven in Christ. No hidden pride working its way upward. No secret self-interest spreading through His motives. No corruption at the root of who He was. He was pure all the way through, like the fine flour that had been sifted until not a single impure grain remained.

Paul draws this connection directly in 1 Corinthians 5:7-8: “Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Therefore let us keep the feast, not with the old leaven, the leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.” Christ is the unleavened bread. And here is a searching truth: the bread we feed on shapes what we become. If we feed on the unleavened Christ His Word, His character, His ways; we are being shaped into lives of sincerity and truth. The bread forms the one who eats it.

Twelve loaves were placed on the table; one representing each tribe of Israel. And every single loaf was exactly the same size. Two-tenths of an ephah of flour each, approximately six pounds per loaf. No tribe received a larger loaf than another. No tribe was represented by a smaller loaf. All twelve stood before the face of Yahweh at the same level, in the same place, with the same weight of representation.

The twelve loaves speak of all of God’s people; every name, every background, every tribe being held before the face of God. Not the faithful tribes only. Not the strong tribes only. All twelve. This is what Christ does:
He presents all His people before the Father, always. Hebrews 7:25 says He always lives to make intercession for them. Ephesians 1:6 says we are “accepted in the Beloved”; our acceptance before God is not based on our own worthiness but on His. Romans 10:12 says there is no distinction before God, bestowing His riches on all who call on Him. Before the face of Yahweh, the table declared that no tribe was above another. Before the face of the Father today, no believer is above another in Christ.

The number twelve also speaks of completeness. Twelve tribes of Israel. Twelve apostles of the new covenant. Twelve foundations of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:14). The twelve loaves were not ten with two missing or eleven with one forgotten. They were twelve; the full covenant number, fully represented, fully present, none absent. Not one person who belongs to Christ is forgotten or missing from the Father’s sight.

On top of each pile of six loaves sat a golden cup filled with pure frankincense; a white, silvery, intensely fragrant gum resin. When the bread was replaced every Sabbath, this frankincense was taken and burned separately on the altar of incense as a memorial fire offering to Yahweh. The bread and the incense served different purposes. The bread was given to be eaten by the priests.
The frankincense was given to ascend to God. The bread was for the people. The incense was for the Father.

The frankincense speaks of the fragrance of Christ’s life ascending before the Father. When the wise men came to worship the infant Christ, they brought three gifts: gold pointing to His kingly character and divine anointing, frankincense pointing to the fragrance of His holy life and priestly ministry, and myrrh pointing to His suffering and death. The frankincense at His birth was a prophetic announcement of what His entire life would be before the Father; a perpetual, rising, sweet fragrance of holiness, obedience, and love. Ephesians 5:2 says He “gave Himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God.” His whole life, not just the cross, was a fragrant offering. Every prayer He prayed, every act of obedience, every compassionate touch, every moment of holiness in an unholy world; frankincense rising to the Father.

The frankincense on the showbread was specifically called a memorial before God (Leviticus 24:7). It was a reminder of the covenant, of the people, of God’s promises. Christ’s intercession before the Father is this same memorial; living, active, perpetual. Romans 8:34 tells us He is at the right hand of God, interceding for us right now. Revelation 8:3-4 shows the prayers of all the saints being offered with incense before the throne, the smoke ascending before God. The frankincense that rose from the table every Sabbath is a picture of the unceasing intercession of Christ rising before the Father on behalf of every person who belongs to Him.

And so the table of showbread, in its entirety, pointed to Christ. Every plank of acacia wood. Every beaten sheet of gold. The double crown around the edges. The fine flour sifted until perfect. The unleavened character without corruption. The twelve equal loaves before the face of God. The frankincense ascending as a fragrance of intercession. All of it; one portrait of the Messiah, drawn in the materials of the wilderness tabernacle, carried on Kohathite shoulders through the desert, waiting for the reality to arrive. Paul said it plainly in Colossians 2:17: the reality is found in Christ. We have now seen how true that is. Tomorrow we will study what it means that we, the new covenant Kohathites, carry this table on our shoulders today.

title: Prayer
text: Great is Yahweh, and greatly to be praised, and His greatness is unsearchable (Psalm 145:3). You placed a table in the wilderness and put a portrait of Your Son on it in bread and gold and frankincense, long before He came. You hid the message in the details, waiting for those who would look closely enough to see it. We thank You for the fine flour; for a character in Christ that was tested by everything and found pure, that went through every grinding and sifting of this world and came out as bread. We thank You for the unleavened loaves; for One in whom there was no hidden corruption, no pride at the root, no leaven spreading unseen through His motives. We thank You that all twelve loaves stand before Your face, that we are held before You in Him, not because of our worthiness but because He carries us there. And we thank You for the frankincense, that even now His intercession rises before You as a fragrance on our behalf, that He is always there, always presenting us, always remembering the covenant. Help us to feed on this bread daily. Help us to carry this portrait of Your Son into the world on our shoulders. In Christ’s name, amen.
